perturbation.original_text: The following terms shall have the respective meanings set forth below throughout this Agreement: "Affiliate" means, with respect to any person, any other person who directly or indirectly, through one or more intermediaries, controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with, such person. The term "control" means the possession, directly or indirectly, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract or otherwise, and the terms "controlled" and "controlling" have meanings correlative thereto. For the avoidance of doubt, for the purposes of this Agreement and all exhibits thereto, the term Affiliate shall not apply to the relationship between RGHI or RGHL or either of their respective Affiliates on the one hand and RCP and its direct and indirect subsidiaries on the other hand.
perturbation.changed_text: The following terms shall have the respective meanings set forth below throughout this Agreement: "Affiliate" means, with respect to any person, any other person who directly or indirectly, through one or more intermediaries, controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with, such person. However, for the purpose of determining service provision obligations under Article II, an Affiliate shall only include entities where the control exceeds 75% ownership. The term "control" means the possession, directly or indirectly, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract or otherwise, and the terms "controlled" and "controlling" have meanings correlative thereto. For the avoidance of doubt, for the purposes of this Agreement and all exhibits thereto, the term Affiliate shall not apply to the relationship between RGHI or RGHL or either of their respective Affiliates on the one hand and RCP and its direct and indirect subsidiaries on the other hand.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a conflicting definition of "Affiliate." The general definition includes any entity under common control, whereas the altered definition in Section 1.1 adds the requirement of 75% ownership for the purpose of determining service provision obligations under Article II. This creates a contradiction because an entity may be an affiliate under the general definition but not for service obligations, leading to disputes over which entities are bound by service requirements. For example, if an entity is 51% owned by RGHI, is it considered an affiliate when RGHI engages, or cause one of their Affiliates to engage, one or more parties (including other third parties or Affiliates) to provide some or all of the Services according to Section 2.2 of this contract? Since Section 2.2 is an Article II section, and the perturbation is specifically stating that it only includes entities where the control exceeds 75% ownership.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Either Party may, in its sole discretion, engage, or cause one of their Affiliates to engage, one or more parties (including other third parties or Affiliates) to provide some or all of the Services; provided, (i) such Party is using such Affiliate or third party to perform the same Services for itself and its Affiliates (to the extent applicable), (ii) such arrangement would not increase the cost to Recipient for such Services, and (iii) if such third party is not already engaged with respect to such Service as of the date hereof, Provider shall obtain the prior written consent of Recipient (not to be unreasonably withheld). Provider shall (x) be responsible for the performance or non-performance of any such parties and (y) in all cases remain responsible for ensuring that obligations with respect to the standards of Services set forth in Article III of this Agreement are satisfied with respect to any Services provided by such Affiliate or third party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a conflicting definition of "Affiliate". The original definition includes any entity under common control, whereas the altered definition adds the requirement of 75% ownership for service provision obligations under Article II. This contradicts the contradicted text, as Section 2.2 allows either party to engage its Affiliates to provide services. The change means that an entity could be an affiliate under the general definition but not for the purposes of service obligations under Article II, creating uncertainty and potential disputes. This creates a direct conflict within the document because an entity with less than 75% control, who were once considered affiliates, may now not be considered affiliates with respect to service provision obligations as mentioned in the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: Provider shall perform the Services (i) at a level of quality substantially similar in all material respects to that at which such Services were performed or enjoyed during the twelve (12) month period prior to the date hereof and (ii) in accordance with applicable Law (collectively, (i) and (ii), the "Service Standard").
perturbation.changed_text: Provider shall perform the Services (i) at a level of quality substantially similar in all material respects to that at which such Services were performed or enjoyed during the twelve (12) month period prior to the date hereof, with reasonable deviations permitted due to unforeseen circumstances, and (ii) in accordance with applicable Law (collectively, (i) and (ii), the "Service Standard").
perturbation.explanation: This change modifies the definition of "Service Standard" in Section 3.1(a) by adding "with reasonable deviations permitted due to unforeseen circumstances". The original definition mandates performance "at a level of quality substantially similar" to the past, without exceptions. The addition introduces ambiguity and potential contradiction with other sections that expect consistent, high-quality service. This change contradicts section 9.4 (b) because the addition on the Service Standard is making the remedies for breaching that service standard less obvious, since unforeseen circumstances might take place that are affecting the original term. It will be more difficult to address if there is a breah.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (b) EXCEPT WITH RESPECT TO A MATERIAL BREACH CONSTITUTING WILLFUL MISCONDUCT BY A PROVIDER, REPEAT PERFORMANCE OF A SERVICE BY THE PROVIDER OR REFUND OF THE FEES PAID FOR A SERVICE SHALL BE THE SOLE AND EXCLUSIVE REMEDY FOR BREACH OF THE SERVICES STANDARD FOR SUCH SERVICE.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces 'reasonable deviations permitted due to unforeseen circumstances' into the 'Service Standard' definition. This directly contradicts the remedies outlined in section (b), which offers 'REPEAT PERFORMANCE OF A SERVICE BY THE PROVIDER OR REFUND OF THE FEES PAID' as the sole remedy for breaching the service standard, except in cases of willful misconduct. The introduced deviation allows the Provider to claim unforeseen circumstances, making it harder to enforce the original remedies and introducing uncertainty about what constitutes a breach of the Service Standard. The original intention of the remedy being the 'sole and exclusive' becomes uncertain as the definition of the standard has been altered, making the remedy no longer straightforward.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall pay Provider (i) the fee for each Service set forth on Exhibit A or Exhibit B, (ii) Providers' and their Affiliates' reasonable and documented out-of-pocket expenses incurred in providing the Services, including the third-party fees and expenses that are charged to Recipient or their Affiliates in connection with provision of the Services (including any fees and expenses charged by subcontractors permitted to provide the Services under Section 2.2) but excluding payments made to employees of Provider or any of their Affiliates pursuant to Section 5.2, and (iii) any other fees as agreed to by the Parties in writing (collectively, the "Fees").
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall pay Provider (i) the fee for each Service set forth on Exhibit A or Exhibit B, (ii) Providers' and their Affiliates' reasonable and documented out-of-pocket expenses incurred in providing the Services, including the third-party fees and expenses that are charged to Recipient or their Affiliates in connection with provision of the Services (including any fees and expenses charged by subcontractors permitted to provide the Services under Section 2.2) but excluding payments made to employees of Provider or any of their Affiliates pursuant to Section 5.2, and (iii) any other fees as agreed to by the Parties in writing, provided that any such additional fees must be pre-approved by the Recipient's CFO (collectively, the "Fees").
perturbation.explanation: This change modifies the definition of “Fees” in Section 5.1 by adding a condition that "any such additional fees must be pre-approved by the Recipient's CFO.” This contradicts section 5.3 because the new additional to Section 5.1 adds another step on what is considered to be "fees" and the conditions arround it. This is affecting section 5.3 due to the fact, that the invoices being made, are subject to the pre-approved fee, which is not being said in this section, and there will be contradiction on what needs to be included in the fees. This creates an internal contradiction about what defines fees, making it unclear what can and cannot be invoiced to the Recipient.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Provider shall submit or cause to be submitted to Recipient in writing, within 15 days after the end of each month, an invoice setting forth the Fees for the Services provided to Recipient during such month in reasonable detail, as applicable, due under such invoice.
perturbation.justification: The change introduces a new requirement for additional fees – pre-approval by the Recipient's CFO – which is not mentioned in the contradicted section (5.3). Section 5.3 states that Provider shall submit invoices setting forth the Fees for the Services provided, but it doesn't include the pre-approval requirement established in the modified definition of 'Fees' in Section 5.1. This creates a contradiction because the invoice submission process in Section 5.3 would implicitly assume that all fees are valid, when in fact additional fees require prior CFO approval according to the changed Section 5.1. This creates uncertainty regarding which fees can be invoiced, creating an ambiguity as to whether the invoice should only include already-approved fees, or if it can include unapproved fees pending approval.
